smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: 3 ads for Barrelhand
thread.body_text:
hey Karel, spent a couple minutes on the Barrelhand site and honestly can't figure out why we're not already working together haha.

anyway, that's not why I'm writing. I run Meta ads for ecom brands, and instead of pitching you some BS, I actually built you a few. (gotta provide value before anything)

built 3 ad mockups from Barrelhand's real products, plus a world-class brand playbook I pulled from your site and reviews. it's done and sitting ready on my end.

want me to send it over?

- Kyle

Hi Kyle, 

 We are currently running meta ads, would be curious what you had in mind and how you would approach it differently.
 

 -Karel

here you go Karel - 3 ad mockups built from Barrelhand's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: Barrelhand sells a $9,750 spaceflight-grade mechanical watch (Monolith) to serious collectors, has no visible reviews and no ads we can see, so this call is about building a considered-purchase funnel from zero, not fixing a broken one.

deep_dive.what_they_are: An engineering-first independent watch brand out of San Francisco. They build ultra-premium mechanical toolwatches aimed at spaceflight and the collector frontier. Their whole story is about tools for the next chapter of space exploration.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Their main product is the Monolith, a mechanical toolwatch priced at $9,750. It comes in three strap colorways: Barrelhand Orange, Black, and Black & Orange. Next batch deliveries start Q4 2026. Orders are fully refundable before shipment. They also have a Memory Disc project and downloadable Monolith source files under their own R&D license. Do not guess at anything beyond this. We could not see a full catalog.
deep_dive.positioning: Top-tier, technical, mission-driven. Not a heritage luxury house. They frame themselves as an engineering-first independent building tools for life beyond Earth. Frontier, not old-money.
deep_dive.who_buys: Serious watch collectors and space-and-tech enthusiasts with high disposable income. They value engineering innovation, provable performance, and being early to a frontier story.
strengths: Strong, distinct brand story and voice. A $9,750 watch built around space exploration is memorable and gives us real angles to work with.
High price point means even a small number of sales is meaningful revenue, so we do not need huge volume to matter.
gaps: No visible reviews or social proof anywhere on the site. For a near ten thousand dollar purchase from an unknown brand, that is a real trust hole.
No Meta ads we can find running. That means we are starting from zero, with no existing winners to build on and no proof yet that paid traffic converts for them.

game_plan.headline: Build a two-step funnel for a high-ticket considered purchase: warm cold audiences with the brand story first, then retarget the interested few toward the Monolith.

phases.goal: Prove that cold traffic will stop, watch, and hand over an email. At this price nobody buys a $9,750 watch on the first click, so month one is about finding an audience that engages and grows the newsletter list.
campaigns: One cold prospecting campaign built around the three ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet, driving to the Monolith page and the newsletter signup.
One small retargeting campaign catching anyone who watched the video or visited the site, pointing them back to the brand story.
lead_products: Monolith. It is the only product we can confirm they sell and the one their whole site is built around, so it is the obvious and only sensible thing to lead with. Lead with the Barrelhand Orange colorway since it is their signature color and the most on-brand visual for stopping the scroll.
phases.creative: Run the three ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet. Lead with the one that carries the brand story hardest, the 'window to the future' angle, because a cold buyer needs to understand who Barrelhand is before price ever matters. The other two rotate in behind it. Every ad leans on their real language: engineered for modern spaceflight, built to withstand the harsh environments of space, your companion for life beyond Earth.
phases.budget: Start small and steady. See estimates below.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Cheap, strong video watch-through and a steady trickle of newsletter signups and product page visits. We are looking for engagement and list growth, not sales yet. One or two sales this early would be a bonus, not the target.

phases.goal: Put more money behind whatever ad and audience won in month one, and widen the cold audience around it. Start turning the warm list and site visitors into actual sales.
campaigns: Scale the winning cold ad and pause the weak ones.
Widen prospecting into new lookalike and interest audiences built off the people who engaged in month one.
Stronger retargeting to site visitors and the newsletter list, this time pushing the offer directly: fully refundable before shipment, next batch Q4 2026, early access.
lead_products: Monolith, still the hero. Test the Black and Black & Orange colorways in retargeting to see which visual pulls the interested crowd best.
phases.creative: Double down on the winning angle from month one and make two or three fresh variations of it. Add retargeting creative that answers the trust question head on and leans on the fully-refundable-before-shipment promise, since that removes the biggest reason a collector hesitates.
phases.budget: Raise spend only on what proved itself. See estimates.
phases.what_success_looks_like: First repeatable sales coming through retargeting, a growing warm audience, and a clear picture of which colorway and which angle convert best.

phases.goal: Scale the proven setup and add a second product to the funnel so we are not resting everything on one watch.
campaigns: Scale the winning cold and retargeting campaigns to the highest spend the margin allows.
Introduce the Memory Disc as a second entry point and a lower-commitment way for space fans to buy into the brand, then retarget those buyers toward the Monolith.
A dedicated retargeting push around the Q4 2026 delivery window as it gets closer, since a real ship date creates honest urgency.
lead_products: Monolith as the main revenue driver. Memory Disc as the new second product, added because it lets us capture people who love the story but are not ready for a ten thousand dollar watch, and warm them up over time.
Note: I do not know the Memory Disc price or whether it is for sale yet. Confirm on the call before we commit to using it.
phases.creative: Keep the proven Monolith ads running. Build a fresh set around the Memory Disc and the 'preserving human artifacts' idea. Use the real Q4 2026 date in urgency messaging, never fake countdowns.
phases.budget: Scale on results only. See estimates.
phases.what_success_looks_like: A steady flow of Monolith sales from a funnel we can turn up or down, plus a second product feeding new people into the top of it.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: At a $9,750 price point almost no one buys on first click, so early spend is for building an engaged audience and a newsletter list, not chasing instant sales. This gets enough daily clicks and video views to learn which ad and audience work without burning money before we know anything.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: I will not put a number on this before the call
estimates.basis: One Monolith sale is $9,750, so revenue is lumpy and a single sale swings the whole month. Without knowing their current sales volume, batch size, and how many units are even available for Q4 2026, any revenue figure would be a guess. I need the numbers in discovery first.

assumptions: The Monolith is actively for sale right now and units are available for the Q4 2026 batch. If it is sold out or pre-order only, the whole funnel goal changes to list-building for the next batch.
Their margin on a $9,750 watch is healthy enough to fund paid ads and absorb a long consideration window. If margin is thin, budgets shrink.
They can handle the returns risk of a fully-refundable offer at this price. If refunds are already a problem, we soften that angle.
The Memory Disc is a real, buyable product with a known price. If it is only a project, we drop it from phase 3.

questions.question: You mentioned you are already running Meta ads. What are you spending a month right now, and who is running them, you or an agency?
questions.listening_for: Real spend level and whether we would be replacing someone. If spend is tiny or there is no clear owner, that explains why we could not find any ads. If an agency runs them, find out what is missing.

questions.question: How many Monoliths do you sell in a typical month, and how many units do you have available for the Q4 2026 batch?
questions.listening_for: Volume and scarcity. This is the number that decides everything. If they only have a handful of units, the goal is a waitlist, not a sales machine.

questions.question: At $9,750, how much room is there in the margin to spend on ads and still come out ahead?
questions.listening_for: Whether the economics support paid ads at all. High-ticket usually has room, but hand-built watches can carry heavy costs. If margin is thin, we scale slower.

questions.question: When you have run ads before, what happened? Did anything ever actually sell from them?
questions.listening_for: Whether paid traffic has ever converted for them. If it never has, we are proving a channel from scratch and should say so. If it has, we build on what worked.

questions.question: When someone lands on your site who has never heard of Barrelhand, what convinces them you are real and worth ten thousand dollars? Do you have any reviews, press, or customer stories?
questions.listening_for: Whether social proof exists somewhere off the site. This is the biggest gap. If they have collector testimonials or press we did not see, we can put it into ads and the whole thing gets easier.

questions.question: How long does a typical buyer take from first hearing about you to actually ordering?
questions.listening_for: The length of the consideration window. A long window means we lean hard on retargeting and email, and month one cannot be judged on sales.

questions.question: What usually stops people from buying? Is it price, trust, the wait until Q4 2026, or something else?
questions.listening_for: The real objection to attack in ad copy. If it is the wait, we lean on early access. If it is trust, we lean on the refundable offer and proof.

questions.question: Is the Memory Disc actually for sale, and what does it cost?
questions.listening_for: Whether we have a real second product for phase 3. If it is just a project, we drop it and rely on Monolith colorways instead.

questions.question: Do you get people searching for you by name, or searching things like space watch or aerospace watch and finding you?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is real search demand. Serious watch collectors do search by brand and by category, so there is likely a genuine Google opening here, especially for people who saw the ads and later search the name. Listen for whether that traffic already exists.

questions.question: When someone Googles Barrelhand right now, what shows up, and does anything about the brand rank at all?
questions.listening_for: Whether they own their own brand search and whether SEO could capture the interest our ads create. If ads drive searches but nothing ranks, that is a clear reason to add Google and basic SEO later.

objections.objection: We are already running Meta ads, so what would you actually do differently?
objections.answer: We could not find any of your ads running publicly, which tells me they are either very small or paused. Either way, the bigger issue is that a $9,750 watch does not sell on a first click. Most brands at this price run ads that go straight for the sale and get nothing. I would build a two-step funnel: warm people up with your story first, then retarget the ones who lean in. That is the difference.

objections.objection: Our watch is $9,750. Nobody buys something like that from a Facebook ad.
objections.answer: Agreed, and that is exactly why I would not try to. The ad's job is not to close the sale. It is to find the small number of collectors who care about this story and get them onto your newsletter and into your world. The sale happens later, through retargeting and email, over the weeks they take to decide. Ads open the door, they do not close it.

objections.objection: We do not have reviews or big social proof yet. Doesn't that make ads pointless?
objections.answer: It makes them harder, not pointless, and it is the first thing I flagged. It is actually a reason to start now. Early ads build the audience and the first customers whose words become your proof. In the meantime we lean on what you do have: the engineering story, the refundable-before-shipment offer, and the frontier positioning. That carries the trust while the reviews catch up.

objections.objection: This is a slow, considered purchase. How will we even know in the first month if it is working?
objections.answer: Fair, and I would not judge month one on sales. Month one proves people stop, watch, and join your list. If that engagement is cheap and your audience is growing, the funnel is working even before the first sale lands. The sales show up in month two and three as those people finish deciding. I will be straight with you about which stage we are at the whole way.

red_flags.flag: No visible reviews or social proof anywhere on the site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Asking a stranger to spend $9,750 with an unknown brand and no proof is hard. We can work around it with the story and the refundable offer, but conversion will be slower and we need to set that expectation up front.

red_flags.flag: No Meta ads we could find running, despite them saying they run ads.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We are effectively starting from zero with no proven winners to build on. That is fine, but it means month one is learning, not scaling, and we should not promise fast results.

red_flags.flag: Very high single price and a Q4 2026 delivery window.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Revenue will be lumpy and the buying decision is long. One sale swings a whole month. Any promise about monthly revenue is risky until we know their unit volume and how many are available.

red_flags.flag: We cannot confirm the Memory Disc is a real buyable product.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Phase 3 leans on it as a second product. If it is only a research project, that part of the plan falls away and we lean on watch colorways instead. Confirm before committing.

other_channel_openings: Google Search for their brand name. Because a $9,750 watch is a considered buy, people who see the Meta ads will very likely go search 'Barrelhand' before ordering. If they do not own that search result with a simple branded campaign, they lose warm buyers at the finish line. Worth raising once we confirm search volume in discovery.

Basic SEO around the brand and product names. Serious watch collectors research heavily before buying, so ranking their own pages for 'Barrelhand Monolith' and related terms would catch interest the ads create. Only worth it if discovery confirms people are searching.
